title: Velocity-driven glitch text on hover
desc: Map smoothed mouse speed to a 0-1 intensity value, then drive slice count, RGB offset, and recovery easing from that single number.
descAr: تحويل سرعة الماوس إلى قيمة شدة 0-1 لتحديد عدد الشرائح وإزاحة RGB وسرعة الاستعادة من رقم واحد.
summary: A per-character glitch animation where corruption intensity, slice count, and recovery easing all scale with how fast the mouse enters the element — instead of playing one fixed effect every time.
problem: Most glitch-on-hover effects play the exact same animation regardless of how the user interacted with the element. A slow, deliberate mouse movement triggers the same chaotic RGB-split-and-shatter sequence as a fast swipe across the screen — there's no relationship between the user's gesture and the visual response. This breaks the illusion the effect is trying to sell. A glitch is supposed to feel like data corruption caused by interference — and interference should scale with the force of whatever caused it. A gentle hover producing a full violent shatter reads as disconnected from the interaction, not premium.
problemTitle: A fixed glitch effect feels robotic
solution: Track the mouse's velocity with exponential smoothing, normalize it into a 0–1 intensity value, and derive every other parameter — slice count, RGB offset, particle count, recovery duration, even which easing curve to use — from that single number. This turns one physical measurement into a coordinated system: a slow approach produces a few large, soft slices and a quick fade-out. A fast swipe produces dozens of thin slices, visible RGB splitting, flying pixel fragments, and an elastic spring-back recovery.
solutionTitle: Map mouse velocity to a single intensity value

callout.text: Key idea: don't hardcode separate “light” and “heavy” animation variants. Derive everything from one continuous variable so the transition between intensities is smooth, not a hard switch.

code.html: const vel = Math.hypot(dx, dy); const speed = lerp(prev, vel, 0.15); const i = clamp((speed-1.5)/26.5, 0, 1); // i drives slices, RGB offset & recovery

codeSteps.label: Track and smooth mouse velocity
codeSteps.html: let smoothVX = 0; let lastMX = 0, lastT = 0; window.addEventListener('mousemove', e => { const now = performance.now(); const dt = Math.max(now - lastT, 1); const rawVX = (e.clientX - lastMX) / dt * 16; smoothVX = lerp(smoothVX, rawVX, 0.40); // exponential smoothing lastMX = e.clientX; lastT = now; });

codeSteps.label: Convert speed into a 0–1 intensity value
codeSteps.html: function getIntensity() { const speed = Math.sqrt(smoothVX ** 2 + smoothVY ** 2); // 1.5 = dead zone (ignores tiny jitter), 26.5 = speed that maps to full intensity return clamp((speed - 1.5) / 26.5, 0, 1); }

codeSteps.label: Derive every visual parameter from intensity
codeSteps.html: const intensity = getIntensity(); const sliceCount = Math.round(lerp(8, 50, intensity)); const maxShift = lerp(3, 42, intensity); const rgbOpacity = lerp(0.08, 0.50, intensity); const recoveryMs = lerp(80, 320, intensity); const easing = intensity < 0.4 ? easeOutCubic : intensity < 0.75 ? easeOutQuint : elasticOut; // spring overshoot only at high speed

baDesc: The structural difference is small in code, but the perceived quality difference is large — the second version makes the element feel physically responsive.

before.desc: Identical result for a slow drift or a fast flick — no gesture awareness.
before.note: Identical result for a slow drift or a fast flick — no gesture awareness.

after.desc: Every parameter scales continuously with how the user actually moved.
after.note: Every parameter scales continuously with how the user actually moved.
